sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE had no effect. Black F at cols 9-17 (left), gray F at cols 45-53 (right), yellow F split on right. All at rows 45-53. LEFT swaps black/gray F positions. Need to find what completes the level.
- Plan: Try RIGHT to swap positions back, then try SPACE. If that doesn't work, try MOUSE or ACTION7.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 194 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 6.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the images, steps 192, 193, and 194 all look the same - the gray F and black F are still separated. RIGHT didn't change anything either.
Let me reconsider the mechanics. Looking at the earlier sequence:
- Step 189: The two F shapes were overlapping
- Step 190: They swapped positions (gray went left, black went right)
- Step 191: They moved further apart
- Step 192: Even further apart
- Step 193: LEFT - no change
- Step 194: RIGHT - no change
So the F shapes seem to have been moving apart automatically, and now they've stopped. LEFT and RIGHT don't do anything now.
Looking at the current state:
- Gray F (c): cols 24-29, rows 45-53
- Black F (B): cols 33-41, rows 45-53
- Yellow F (Y): on the right edge
Maybe I need to try MOUSE or ACTION7. Or maybe SPACE actually does something I haven't noticed.
Let me look at the segmentation more carefully. The white pixel at [52, 37] (or [52, 40] before) - that's a single white pixel. That might be a cursor or indicator.
Let me try clicking on the yellow F with MOUSE, or try ACTION7.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. The images show:
1. A blue background
2. A light blue vertical strip in the center
3. Three F-shaped objects at the bottom
4. A yellow strip on the right edge
The F shapes seem to be the main interactive elements. Maybe the goal is to get all three F shapes to overlap? Or to match them in some way?
Let me try SPACE again, or try MOUSE on one of the F shapes.
Actually, looking more carefully at the data:
- The gray F is at cols 24-29
- The black F is at cols 33-41 (with the white pixel at [52, 37] inside it)
- The yellow F is on the right edge
The white pixel might be a cursor. Let me try moving it with LEFT/RIGHT to position it on the yellow F, then SPACE to activate something.
Wait, the white pixel moved from col 40 to col 37 when I pressed RIGHT. That's counterintuitive - pressing RIGHT moved the white pixel to the left. Let me check if pressing LEFT moves it to the right.
Actually, looking at the data more carefully:
- Before RIGHT: W at [52, 40]
- After RIGHT: W at [52, 37]
So RIGHT moved the white pixel 3 columns to the left. That's strange. Let me try LEFT to see if it moves right.
Actually wait - I need to reconsider. The black F shape also changed:
- Before: boundary at cols 33-42
- After: boundary at cols 33-39
And the gray F changed too:
- Before: cols 21-29
- After: cols 24-29
So the shapes are changing, not just the white pixel. This is confusing. Let me look at the images more carefully.
Looking at the images, steps 192, 193, and 194 all look visually identical. The segmentation data shows changes but the visual appearance is the same. This might be because the segmentation is picking up subtle differences in the background or something.
Let me just try SPACE and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: RIGHT also had no visible effect on the F shapes. The white pixel (cursor?) moved slightly. The F shapes seem stuck in their current positions. Let me try SPACE or MOUSE to interact with the objects directly.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state and try SPACE
curr = current_frame
nodes = curr.segmentation['nodes']
print("Current objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:  # Skip large background objects
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
